skippedSources.name: The Founders Corner
skippedSources.reason: OKRs vs KPIs management framework — no investment or market thesis; primarily a book promotion vehicle with no original data or claims

items.title: US crude to all-time low: 213M bbl draw by July — largest in history
items.idea: HFIR's April 25 prediction is tracking: US total liquids with SPR will fall to an ALL-TIME LOW (never below 1.4B bbls since EIA records began in 1990). Since April 25, US total liquids dropped 48.846M bbls at 16.3M bbls/week vs predicted 18M — on pace. The draw from now to end of July is the largest and fastest in history, regardless of what happens at the Strait of Hormuz.
items.angle: The draw is baked in. Even a Hormuz deal tomorrow doesn't stop the inventory collapse. This changes the crude vs product spread dynamics — refiners will compete for tightening crude supply into peak driving season.
items.tweetA: HFIR called this on April 25. US total liquids with SPR is on pace to hit an ALL-TIME LOW by end of July — a level never breached since EIA started tracking in 1990. 48.8M bbls drawn in 3 weeks. 16.3M/week. The math doesn't lie. $USO $CL_F
items.tweetB: The Hormuz ceasefire debate is a distraction. US crude inventories are drawing at 16+ million bbls/week regardless. By end of July: all-time low. This is happening. The only question is whether the market prices it before or after it shows up in the EIA report.

charts.desc: US Total Liquids with SPR trajectory chart showing draw pace vs April 25 forecast; US product storage vs 2022 lows — CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: 3 weeks until US petroleum product exports stop — the world's buffer disappears
items.idea: US product storage is now only ~13M bbls from the lows seen in Q3 2022. HFIR says approximately 3 weeks until US petroleum product exports fall. Once that buffer is gone, the elevated petroleum product exports that the world has been enjoying disappear. Refinery throughput picked up but crude draws remain very heavy.
items.angle: The 2022 analog: crack spreads went parabolic when product storage collapsed. We are approaching the same threshold. Watch crack spreads vs crude — the product market will price this first.
items.tweetA: In 2022, product storage collapse sent crack spreads to ATH. US product storage is now 13M bbls from those same lows. Refinery runs are at max. HFIR says ~3 weeks until US product exports stop. The world's backup tank is almost empty. $VLO $MPC $PSX
items.tweetB: The market is focused on Hormuz and crude. The real trade is product. US petroleum product exports are about to fall — not eventually, in weeks. When that flow stops, every importer that's been relying on US excess will have to scramble.

charts.desc: US product storage vs 2022 lows chart

items.title: Hantavirus: R0 2.7 on ship, 40% fatality rate — speculator hedges oil delta, keeps ags
items.idea: A hantavirus outbreak originated on a cruise ship (R0=2.7 onboard). A macro speculator hedged ~15% of oil book in futures (delta-neutral) while keeping calls for vol, reasoning that ship R0 2.7 implies population R0 ~0.5 (using 5x hot/cold COVID ratio), below replacement and likely self-extinguishing. BUT: virus is ~50x more deadly than COVID (~40% fatality vs ~1%), while COVID was ~7x more contagious. WHO's last update on a disease in 23 countries was 6 days ago.
items.angle: Live tail risk. Base case is containment, but WHO silence + 40% fatality + no airborne confirmation = genuine uncertainty. Oil options skew may not be pricing this. Agricultural positions held — logical since lockdowns hit fuel harder than food.
items.tweetA: There's a hantavirus outbreak. Ship R0: 2.7. Fatality rate: ~40%. That's 50x deadlier than COVID, 7x less contagious. The math probably keeps it contained. But the WHO hasn't updated in 6 days on a disease in 23 countries with a 40% kill rate. 'Probably contained' is doing a lot of work right now.
items.tweetB: Smart macro trade from Campbell Ramble: sold ~15% of oil delta (futures) against oil calls. Keep the vol, drop the direction. Keep ags — lockdowns don't stop food production the same way they stop fuel demand. This is how you think through tail risk that has not been priced.

charts.desc: R0 scatter chart comparing COVID vs hantavirus on infectiousness/deadliness axes

items.title: Claude refused to help research hantavirus — AI safety filters as deliberate information voids
items.idea: The author tried to use Claude (Opus and Sonnet), GPT, and Grok to research hantavirus epidemiology. Claude immediately hit safety filters; after repeated prompts accused the user of 'social engineering' even after acknowledging it was probably overcalibrated. Conclusion: the information vacuum that makes a trade work also made it impossible to research using AI. 'The notion that you can trust machines to do work that doesn't matter but can't rely on them when it does' is a profound failure mode.
items.angle: Underappreciated cost of AI overcalibration: deliberate information voids precisely when stakes are highest. For market participants, AI becomes unreliable at worst moments. Product risk, not just safety feature.
items.tweetA: Buried in the hantavirus note: Campbell spent hours getting Claude to make epidemiology charts. Claude accused him of social engineering. For researching a publicly available R0 number. The safety filter problem isn't just annoying — it creates information voids at exactly the moment you need information most.
items.tweetB: Don't trust machines when it matters, trust them when it doesn't. That's the implicit message from Claude's hantavirus refusal. If your AI tool won't help you model an outbreak with 40% fatality, you have a product liability problem masquerading as a safety feature.

items.title: PPI shock: +1.4% MoM vs +0.5% est; 6.0% YoY — pipeline inflation still loading
items.idea: April PPI +1.4% MoM (vs +0.5% est), 6.0% YoY — highest since Dec 2022 ex-pandemic. Core PPI +1.0% MoM (vs +0.4% est); ex-food/energy/trade +4.4% YoY. Final demand energy +7.8% MoM; gasoline +15.6%; services +1.2% MoM (biggest since March 2022). Kalshi rate-hike-before-2027 odds jump to 35%; 10Y hits 4.48% (2026 high); 20Y and 30Y top 5%. PPI at 6% vs CPI at 3.8% = pipeline still loading.
items.angle: 220bps PPI-CPI gap has to close — either CPI catches up or margins compress or both. Wholesale margins +2.7% shows tariff pass-through beginning. Bond market is pricing it correctly; equities have not.
items.tweetA: PPI 6.0% YoY. CPI 3.8% YoY. That 220bps gap has to close. Either inflation accelerates to consumers or margins collapse. The market is bidding semis to new highs on the same day. One of these trades is wrong. The bond market — 10Y at 2026 high of 4.48% — knows which one.
items.tweetB: April PPI +1.4% MoM vs +0.5% expected. Services component biggest jump since March 2022. This is not just energy — the pipeline is loading. Warsh just became Fed chair. He walks into 6% PPI on Day 1. 'Data dependent' just got complicated.

items.title: Cumulative Hormuz supply loss tops 1 BILLION barrels — Aramco says mid-June is the deadline
items.idea: Cumulative supply loss from Hormuz closure tops 1 billion barrels per Eurasia Group. Aramco CEO Nasser: if reopening slips past mid-June, normalization pushed to 2027. Trump's first request to Xi: help reopen the Strait. WTI ~$101, Brent ~$107.
items.angle: 1 billion barrels = ~10 days of global consumption wiped from supply. If mid-June is the real deadline and no deal exists, the 2027 normalization case puts a floor under energy the market has not fully priced.
items.tweetA: 1 billion barrels. That's how much supply has been lost from the Hormuz closure per Eurasia Group. Aramco's CEO says if it's not reopened by mid-June, normalization slips to 2027. Trump flew to Beijing and his first ask was: help me reopen the Strait. $CL_F $BNO
items.tweetB: Hormuz has been closed long enough to pull 1 billion barrels of supply off the market. HFIR is tracking US inventories heading to all-time lows. Aramco's CEO says mid-June is the deadline. WTI at $101. I don't think this is the top.

items.title: Trump breaks Six Assurances: Taiwan arms on Beijing summit agenda; Iran ceasefire on 'life support'
items.idea: Trump said he will discuss Taiwan arms sales with Xi — breaking the Reagan-era Six Assurances. Iran ceasefire on 'life support'; Trump aides pushing to resume combat operations. Israel struck Lebanon (15 killed). Putin visits Beijing days after Trump departs.
items.angle: Six Assurances breach is historically significant and underreported. If Trump is trading Taiwan arms commitments for Hormuz reopening, that is a massive geopolitical restructuring. Taiwan equity risk is suddenly live.
items.tweetA: Trump said he'll discuss Taiwan arms sales with Xi. That breaks the Six Assurances — a commitment every US president since Reagan has honored. If Hormuz reopening is the ask, Taiwan's defense is the currency. $EWT $TSM
items.tweetB: Beijing this week: Trump arrives with Cook, Musk, and Jensen Huang. Putin arrives days later. China is hosting both sides of the Iran war within the same week. This is not neutrality — this is China positioning as the indispensable broker. Beijing's leverage is at peak.

items.title: Kevin Warsh confirmed as Fed chair — walks in to 6% PPI, 35% hike odds, negative real wages
items.idea: Senate confirmed Warsh as Fed chair; Powell's term ends Friday. Warsh inherits 6.0% PPI, core ex-food/energy/trade at 4.4% YoY, 10Y at 4.48%, Kalshi hike-before-2027 probability at 35%. Nationwide raised May CPI forecast above 4%. Real wages turned negative for first time since April 2023. Trump: 'as soon as this war is over, you'll see inflation go down probably to 1.5%'
items.angle: Warsh is hawkish but faces a market priced for expansion and a president promising deflation post-war. The credibility test comes immediately. Bond vol should reprice.
items.tweetA: Kevin Warsh walks into the Fed chair role on Friday. First data point: PPI 6.0%, core 4.4%, real wages negative, 20Y bond at 5%. Trump is simultaneously promising 1.5% inflation 'once the war ends.' The new chair's first move will define his tenure.
items.tweetB: Warsh is hawkish. He inherits 6% PPI. His mandate comes from a president who publicly promises deflation post-war. That's not a position — it's a trap. Market is pricing 35% chance of a hike before 2027. I'd bet that goes higher.

items.title: Cerebras IPO prices $185 vs $150-160 range, 30x demand — Arm and SoftBank both tried to acquire weeks before
items.idea: Cerebras priced at $185/share (vs $150-160 range), raising $5.55B, ~$50B fully diluted. Both Arm and SoftBank made acquisition attempts weeks before IPO. CEO Feldman stake $1.9B; Brockman 78K shares ($14.4M); Altman $16.5M. 30x+ demand. Trades as $CBRS Thursday.
items.angle: Arm and SoftBank both wanted this company and could not close. That signals what smart money thinks is coming in AI inference. Wafer-scale chips = most credible architectural alternative to Nvidia GPU clusters for certain workloads.
items.tweetA: Cerebras IPO: $185/share vs $150-160 range. $50B valuation. $5.55B raised. 30x oversubscribed. And both Arm AND SoftBank tried to acquire them weeks before listing. When two of the smartest chip investors couldn't buy it, it went public instead. $CBRS
items.tweetB: Arm and SoftBank tried to buy Cerebras. Both failed. So Cerebras went public at $50B with 30x demand. The wafer-scale architecture is the most credible alternative to GPU clusters for AI inference. The valuation mania is real — but so is the technology.

items.title: Anthropic implied valuation hits $1.6T on tokenized crypto venues — while warning tokens may be invalid
items.idea: Anthropic implied valuation hit $1.6T on Ventuals/PreStocks crypto venues. Both Anthropic and OpenAI warned that pre-IPO tokenized shares 'may be invalid.' Ventuals + PreStocks combined volume tripled YTD. Citi reiterated $300 PT on Nvidia, forecasting $80B revenue for May 20 earnings.
items.angle: Tokenized pre-IPO market pricing Anthropic at $1.6T while the company disclaims those tokens. Either genuine pricing of future cash flows or pure speculation vehicle. When companies explicitly disclaim instruments being traded on their behalf, cleanup is usually messy.
items.tweetA: Anthropic is worth $1.6 trillion on crypto pre-IPO venues. Anthropic says those tokens 'may be invalid.' Ventuals volume tripled YTD. This is the new dot-com secondary market — except the underlying companies are explicitly disclaiming the instruments. $ETH
items.tweetB: Private AI valuations on crypto venues: Anthropic $1.6T. Tokenized Treasuries $15B. Cerebras just IPO'd at $50B. None of this existed 2 years ago. Capital market plumbing being rebuilt in real-time with zero regulatory clarity. When the music stops, it stops fast.

items.title: Copper-gold ratio breaks above 200DMA — historically precedes BTC rallies
items.idea: Copper-gold ratio broke above its 200DMA — historically precedes BTC rallies. BTC dipped below $80,000 post-PPI (trading $80,611). Bitcoin bull/bear cycle indicator turned green for first time since March 2023. Strategy holds 818K BTC ($64B).
items.angle: Copper/gold above 200DMA = macro risk-on. Combined with bull/bear indicator green for first time in 3 years, a confluence of signals. PPI-driven dip to $80K could be the entry if macro turns with Hormuz resolution.
items.tweetA: Copper-gold ratio just broke its 200DMA. Historically that precedes BTC rallies. Bitcoin bull/bear indicator flipped green for first time since March 2023. BTC is at $80K, dipping on PPI shock. If you're waiting for a 'cleaner' entry in a bull market, this is usually the moment you don't get it.
items.tweetB: Two signals converging on BTC: copper-gold above 200DMA (macro risk-on) + bull/bear cycle indicator green for first time in 3 years. Strategy sits on 818K BTC. Price at $80K, pressured by PPI. The macro setup for BTC is better than the price action implies.

items.title: Tokenized Treasuries hit $15B AUM; Schwab launches US spot crypto retail trading
items.idea: Tokenized US Treasuries crossed $15B AUM. Charles Schwab launched US spot BTC/ETH retail trading. Kraken parent + Franklin Templeton building on-chain investment products. Ethereum Foundation rolled out Clear Signing anti-drainer standard.
items.angle: Schwab launching spot crypto is a structural legitimization moment. $15B tokenized Treasuries is institutional plumbing building in real-time. Both trends point to the same endpoint: financial system backend migrates to on-chain rails.
items.tweetA: Charles Schwab — the largest US brokerage — just launched spot BTC/ETH retail trading. Tokenized Treasuries: $15B. Franklin Templeton and Kraken building on-chain products. This isn't crypto speculation. This is the financial system's backend migrating rails.
items.tweetB: Tokenized Treasuries $15B. Schwab spot crypto live. Franklin Templeton + Kraken on-chain products announced. Every week another pillar of TradFi moves on-chain. The question isn't whether this happens. It's whether you're positioned before the legacy players finish their migration.

items.title: Real wages turn negative for first time since April 2023 — services inflation now embedded
items.idea: Real wages turned negative for first time since April 2023, driven by sticky services inflation. Final demand services PPI +1.2% MoM — biggest since March 2022. Nationwide raised May CPI forecast above 4%, would top May 2023 high. Negative real wages + rising services PPI = consumer squeeze arriving in real-time.
items.angle: Negative real wages are the transmission mechanism from PPI to consumer behavior. Setup for demand destruction. Watch consumer discretionary and housing for first cracks.
items.tweetA: Real wages just went negative for the first time since April 2023. Services PPI +1.2% in one month — biggest jump since March 2022. The inflation isn't in oil anymore. It's in services, where it's sticky. The squeeze on the US consumer is no longer a forecast. It's happening.
items.tweetB: The sequence: oil shock → PPI 6% → services inflation embeds → real wages negative → consumer demand cracks. We're at step 4. Short-end rates markets are pricing 35% hike odds before 2027. But consumer stress could force the opposite. Most uncomfortable macro setup in years.

items.title: China hosts both Trump and Putin within days — positioning as indispensable broker
items.idea: Trump arrived in Beijing with Cook, Musk, and Jensen Huang. Putin visits Beijing days after Trump departs. Bessent and China VP He Lifeng held 3-hour pre-summit talks at Incheon. China maintaining 10% global tariffs heading into the meeting.
items.angle: China hosting both sides of the Iran conflict's main patrons within one week is not coincidence. Beijing positions as indispensable mediator — its own self-interest (world's largest oil importer) aligns with brokering a Hormuz deal.
items.tweetA: This week: Trump arrives in Beijing with $12T in US market cap in tow, asking Xi to reopen Hormuz. Putin arrives days later. China is hosting both leaders simultaneously. Beijing is not neutral — it is the indispensable broker for the most consequential supply disruption since 1973.
items.tweetB: Why would Xi host both Trump AND Putin within days? Because Hormuz is costing China too — as the world's largest oil importer. China needs the Strait reopened as much as anyone. The summit may actually produce something. Beijing's self-interest aligns with a deal.

items.title: Nifty Fifty parallel: top 10 = 40% of S&P 500; VIX rising WITH equities — the health warning
items.idea: 1972 Nifty Fifty: 45% of S&P 500 across diverse industries. Today's top 10 (all tech except Berkshire): ~40% with far greater concentration. Capital Economics' Reilly flags rare VIX/S&P 500 lockstep — only happened before Volmageddon 2018 and the software selloff late last year. VanEck Semiconductor ETF +60% since late-March trough. Bob Elliott: 'hard to make a common-sense case that the outlook has swung so swiftly for a sector of such size.'
items.angle: VIX rising with price is the canary. Retail buying at 'Covid-era extremes' with simultaneous tail hedge demand = unstable rally fingerprint. This doesn't tell you WHEN — it tells you something is fragile.
items.tweetA: In 1972, the Nifty Fifty had 45% of the S&P 500 — but across diverse industries. Today the top 10 — all tech — have 40%. Semis +60% in 6 weeks. VIX rising WITH the market. This is exactly the fingerprint Authers describes from Volmageddon 2018. It doesn't tell you WHEN. It tells you something is fragile.
items.tweetB: VIX is rising WITH the S&P 500. That's happened only a handful of times in decades — Volmageddon 2018, last year's software selloff. Retail options buying at COVID-era extremes. Everyone is chasing AND hedging simultaneously. That's not a healthy rally. That's a crowded one.

charts.desc: Bridgeway Capital Nifty Fifty post-bubble underperformance chart; VIX/S&P 500 lockstep chart — CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Grantham: 'earnings are not sacred' — AI companies will eventually compete against each other
items.idea: Jeremy Grantham: mega-cap AI profits face pressure from data-center buildout costs, then from AI models competing against each other. Head-to-head AI competition damages profits. Seth Cogswell: 'Nvidia handing customers money to buy their products doesn't make sense — the ecosystem right now is all being subsidized.'
items.angle: Bull case Achilles heel: every dollar of AI revenue is captured by a few companies because there is no real competition yet. When competition arrives, margin compression is certain. The ecosystem subsidy is temporary.
items.tweetA: Grantham: 'earnings are not sacred.' The AI models being built now will eventually compete against each other. Head-to-head competition compresses margins — great for consumers, brutal for companies. The current AI earnings are real. The current AI margins may not survive the next phase.
items.tweetB: Seth Cogswell: 'Nvidia handing customers money to buy their products doesn't make sense — unless it's the only way to keep it going.' The entire AI ecosystem is subsidized. That's the early-phase playbook. The question is what happens when the subsidies stop. History says they always do.

items.title: Electronics components +27% YoY — AI memory shortage ends 40 years of declining compute costs
items.idea: Oxford Economics' Zwemmer: electronics components and accessories +8% MoM in April; electronic components +27% YoY — AI demand created memory chip shortage. Ends decades of declining costs. Bloomberg's non-energy commodities index at all-time high. PCE deflator heading to 3.8% — far too high for rate cuts.
items.angle: Electronics declining was a 40-year secular disinflationary force. AI demand reversed it in 12 months. This flows through to every industry embedding electronics — auto, industrial, consumer, defense. Structural change, not a cycle.
items.tweetA: Electronics components +27% YoY. Memory chips in shortage because of AI demand. For 40 years, electronics got cheaper every year — a secular disinflationary force embedded in almost every product. AI just reversed it in 12 months. This shows up in PPI. Then CPI. Then margins everywhere.
items.tweetB: PCE deflator heading to 3.8% per Oxford Economics. The driver isn't just oil — electronics components +27% YoY and non-energy commodities at ALL-TIME HIGH. Forty years of declining electronics costs were a tailwind for margins. AI demand just turned it into a headwind.

charts.desc: Bloomberg non-energy commodities index at all-time high chart referenced

items.title: UK's Starmer in terminal trouble — gilt yields above Liz Truss era but dysfunction already priced
items.idea: Starmer in severe political difficulty; gilt yields slightly higher than when Truss was ejected in 2022. But Authers argues dysfunction is 'already in the price' — Polymarket has had >50% probability of Starmer leaving by Dec 31 for months. Real driver of UK gilts is oil price; UK more economically exposed to Iran war than US. Gilt/Treasury spread moved almost directly with oil price since hostilities began.
items.angle: Political noise vs actual risk. UK political chaos is expected and not incremental market risk. The actual risk is the Iran war's oil price impact on UK current account. Framework applicable to other geopolitically-exposed markets.
items.tweetA: UK gilts are above Liz Truss levels — but Authers says Starmer's collapse is already priced. The real driver of UK bonds isn't Westminster chaos. It's the Iran war. The gilt/Treasury spread has tracked oil price almost 1:1 since hostilities began. Political noise does not equal market risk.
items.tweetB: Polymarket had >50% odds of Starmer leaving by year-end for months. Markets don't react to expected events. What they DO react to: oil. The UK's bond spread vs US has moved almost directly with oil since the Iran conflict started. The political story is a distraction from the energy trade.

charts.desc: UK gilt yields vs Truss era and gilt/Treasury spread vs oil price correlation

items.title: Semis grew from 4% to 16% of S&P 500 since ChatGPT — treating them as cyclicals is the wrong frame
items.idea: Since ChatGPT launch in late 2022, PHLX semiconductor index grew from 4% to 16% of S&P 500 market cap. Old habit of treating semis as cyclical commodities needs updating. Even older chips now appreciating as assets. 'The part no one really bet on for 2025/2026 was the acute shortages' per Kelly Littlepage of OneChronos.
items.angle: 4% to 16% of S&P 500 in 3 years is one of the fastest sector re-ratings in modern history. Cyclical reversion thesis may simply be wrong if AI demand is multi-year and supply structurally constrained.
items.tweetA: Semis went from 4% to 16% of S&P 500 market cap since ChatGPT. In 3 years. The consensus still calls them 'cyclicals.' The consensus is pricing in a reversion that may not come — because the demand is structural, not cyclical. $SMH $NVDA
items.tweetB: Even old chips are now appreciating as assets. That's the tell this isn't a normal cycle. Acute shortages in 2025-2026 that 'nobody really bet on.' Every time history ended deflation in a key input — oil in 1973, labor in the 1960s — the adjustment took years. We may be early innings on chips.

items.title: CME launches compute futures — compute officially becomes a commodity with a futures curve
items.idea: CME Group announced a futures market to trade compute. CEO Terry Duffy: 'compute is the new oil of the 21st century.' OneChronos (designed with Nobel laureate Paul Milgrom) building a smart market for compute hedging. Companies increasingly looking to hedge compute costs as a business input.
items.angle: The moment a commodity gets a futures market, risk management professionalizes rapidly. A liquid compute futures curve would change capex planning, AI startup economics, and hyperscaler margin conversations.
items.tweetA: CME just launched compute futures. 'Compute is the new oil of the 21st century.' When an input cost gets a futures curve, the professional risk management community has arrived. AI startups can hedge compute costs forward. Hyperscalers can lock in margins. This is infrastructure.
items.tweetB: OneChronos — backed by Nobel-winning auction theorist Paul Milgrom — is building a smart market for compute alongside CME's compute futures launch. Two parallel tracks converging on the same problem. The compute market is professionalizing fast.

items.title: Jensen Huang on Air Force One — Trump called and had him picked up in Alaska mid-flight to Beijing
items.idea: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang not initially included in Trump's China delegation. After media reported a 'Home Alone' moment, Trump called Huang directly and Air Force One stopped in Anchorage to pick him up. Huang now part of delegation with Cook, Musk and others representing $12T+ in US market cap. Agenda: chip trade, Hormuz, Taiwan.
items.angle: Air Force One pickup signals the actual centrality of semiconductors to Trump's China strategy. Nvidia's China position risk is now explicitly geopolitical. Either a deal opens Nvidia's China business or this trip sets the final terms of decoupling.
items.tweetA: Trump called Jensen Huang personally and had Air Force One stop in Alaska to pick him up for the Beijing summit. The fact that the most important chips CEO in the world wasn't originally invited — and then was — tells you everything about how the Nvidia China story is playing out.
items.tweetB: Jensen Huang is in Beijing on Air Force One. The delegation represents $12T+ in US market cap. The agenda: chips, Hormuz, Taiwan. Huang's presence means Nvidia's China H20 chip sales are ON the table. Either a deal gets done and Nvidia's China business opens — or this trip sets the final terms of decoupling.

items.title: 1970s analog: income inequality changes inflation transmission — top 1% wealth holders drive it now, not wage earners
items.idea: BowTieBull's 1970s vs today comparison: (1) 'Safe' companies are completely different in digital era. (2) Everything happens with a lag — crypto didn't move in 2020 until the wealth effect took hold. (3) Income inequality is MASSIVELY different — focus on what the top 1% wealth holders will do, not top-paid employees. Their spending and inflation transmission is fundamentally different.
items.angle: 1970s inflation was a wage-price spiral. Today's inflation transmission runs through asset prices → wealth effect → luxury/discretionary spending by top 1%. The Fed's rate hike tool works on wage earners. It works less well on wealth holders. That asymmetry is underpriced.
items.tweetA: Here's what the 1970s analog gets wrong: then, inflation was a wage-price spiral. Now, income inequality means transmission runs through asset prices → wealth effect → spending by the top 1%. The Fed's rate hike tool works on wage earners. It works less well on wealth holders. That asymmetry is underpriced.
items.tweetB: BowTieBull: 'Do not think about what a top-paid employee will do. Think about what the top 1% wealth holders will do.' When the top 1% own ~35% of all financial assets, inflation analysis that focuses on wages is looking at the wrong input. The wealth effect IS the inflation channel now.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for 1970s vs today asset performance comparison

items.title: PTJ durability framework: proof, feeling, instinct, risk tolerance — the four-part conviction lens
items.idea: Paper Alfa presents Paul Tudor Jones as a reference point for building trading conviction. Key framework: a four-part lens — proof, feeling, instinct, and tolerance for risk. The thesis: durability across market regimes is built by internalizing a way of seeing uncertainty, not by replicating trades. 'Markets don't reward purity of intention. They reward durability.' PTJ survived by adapting without drifting.
items.angle: In today's contradictory setup (6% PPI + record Nasdaq + active war + new Fed chair + hantavirus tail), the durable trader response is not a big bet — it's sizing smaller and preserving optionality. The conviction lens says: intuition first, proof as confirmation.
items.tweetA: Paper Alfa on PTJ: 'Markets don't reward purity of intention. They reward durability.' We're in the most contradictory macro setup in years — record Nasdaq, 6% PPI, new Fed chair, active war, hantavirus tail. The durable trader response isn't a big bet. It's staying in the game.
items.tweetB: PTJ's conviction lens: proof, feeling, instinct, risk tolerance. Feeling and instinct come before sizing — rational analysis confirms or rejects what intuition already detected. In today's market, my instinct says the bond market is more right than equities. The proof is still loading.
